title: Intestinal Nematode Infections
authors: David J. Diemert, Thomas B. Nutman

items.title: Global Burden and Impact of Intestinal Nematode Infections
items.content: Over one billion individuals worldwide are infected with intestinal nematodes, predominantly in regions lacking adequate sanitation. These infections contribute to malnutrition, impaired development, and reduced work capacity but may offer some protection against allergic diseases.
keyPoints: More than one billion individuals worldwide are infected with intestinal nematodes.
Common in areas with inadequate sanitation, especially in resource-limited tropical and subtropical regions.
Contributors to malnutrition, impaired physical and cognitive development, and diminished work capacity.
May protect some individuals from allergic diseases.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.title: Transmission and Clinical Manifestations of Intestinal Nematodes
items.content: Intestinal nematodes have complex life cycles, with some capable of direct human-to-human transmission. Clinical disease typically arises from repeated exposure to the parasite in its infectious stage, often leading to eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels.
keyPoints: Life cycles are complex; some species can be transmitted directly between humans (e.g., Strongyloides stercoralis, Enterobius vermicularis).
Others require a soil phase for development (e.g., Ascaris lumbricoides, Trichuris trichiura, hookworms).
Clinical disease usually develops with repeated exposure and is related to infection intensity.
Eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE levels are common features, especially when tissue migration occurs.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.title: Understanding Ascariasis Life Cycle and Transmission
items.content: Ascariasis is caused by A. lumbricoides, the largest intestinal nematode in humans, which can reach up to 40 cm in length. The life cycle involves ingestion of infective eggs from contaminated soil, larval migration through the lungs, and maturation into adult worms in the intestines. Eggs are expelled in feces by mature female worms, producing over 200,000 eggs daily.
keyPoints: A. lumbricoides can reach up to 40 cm in length.
Each mature female worm produces over 200,000 eggs per day.
Eggs become infective after several weeks in warm, moist soil and remain so for years.
Time from infection to egg detection is typically 2-3 months.
Adult worms live for 1–2 years.

items.title: Recognizing Clinical Features of Ascariasis
items.content: Clinical manifestations depend on the phase and severity of infection. During larval migration in the lungs, patients may experience cough, substernal discomfort, dyspnea, blood-tinged sputum, and fever. Eosinophilia is common during this phase. In heavy intestinal infections, symptoms can include pain, small-bowel obstruction, or complications like perforation.
keyPoints: Lung phase occurs ~9-12 days after egg ingestion with cough and substernal discomfort.
Eosinophilia develops during the lung phase and subsides over weeks.
Heavy infections can cause intestinal obstruction more common in children.
Complications include biliary colic, cholecystitis, pancreatitis, and intrahepatic abscesses.

items.title: Diagnosing Ascariasis: Laboratory Findings and Imaging
items.content: Ascariasis is diagnosed primarily through microscopic detection of Ascaris eggs in fecal samples, with PCR increasingly used. During the early pulmonary phase, larvae may be found in sputum or gastric aspirates. Eosinophilia is prominent initially but decreases as infection stabilizes. Imaging can reveal worms in the gastrointestinal tract or biliary system.
keyPoints: Characteristic Ascaris eggs are 65 × 45 μm and detected microscopically in feces.
PCR of worm DNA from stool is an emerging diagnostic tool.
Eosinophilia is prominent during early transpulmonary migration.
Imaging may show worms in the GI tract or biliary system.

items.title: Treatment of Ascariasis and Hookworm Infections
items.content: Ascariasis should be treated to prevent complications, using medications like albendazole, mebendazole, pyrantel pamoate, ivermectin, or moxidectin. These are contraindicated in pregnancy. For hookworm infections, treatment is necessary when symptoms such as iron-deficiency anemia and hypoproteinemia occur.
keyPoints: Effective treatments for ascariasis include albendazole (400 mg once), mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days or 500 mg once), pyrantel pamoate (11 mg/kg once, max 1 g), ivermectin (150–200 µg/kg once), and moxidectin (8 mg once).
These medications are contraindicated in pregnancy.
Hookworm infections can lead to iron-deficiency anemia and hypoproteinemia, especially with heavy worm burden or inadequate iron intake.

items.title: Management of Partial Intestinal Obstruction in Ascariasis
items.content: Partial intestinal obstruction due to ascariasis should be managed conservatively with nasogastric suction, IV fluids, and piperazine. Complete obstructions require surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Partial intestinal obstruction from ascariasis is managed with nasogastric suction, IV fluid administration, and piperazine instillation.
Complete obstruction necessitates immediate surgical intervention.

items.title: Diagnosis of Hookworm Infections
items.content: Hookworm infections are diagnosed by identifying characteristic 40 x 60 µm oval eggs in feces. Stool concentration techniques may be needed for light infections, and PCR can differentiate species but is not widely available.
keyPoints: Characteristic hookworm eggs measure 40 x 60 µm and are found in feces.
Stool concentration techniques help detect light infections.
PCR improves species-specific diagnosis but is primarily a research tool.

items.title: Treatment Options for Hookworm Infections and Emerging Drug Resistance
items.content: Hookworm infections are typically treated with anthelmintic drugs such as albendazole or mebendazole. However, there is growing concern about the reduced effectiveness of benzimidazoles against human hookworms.
keyPoints: Albendazole dosage: 400 mg once daily for 3 days
Mebendazole dosages: 500 mg once daily or 100 mg twice daily for 3 days
Concern about reduced effectiveness of benzimidazoles against human hookworms

items.title: Ancylostoma caninum and Human Eosinophilic Enteritis
items.content: In rare cases, Ancylostoma caninum, a canine hookworm, causes human eosinophilic enteritis. This zoonotic infection is characterized by adult hookworms attaching to the small intestine, causing abdominal pain and local eosinophilia.
keyPoints: Ancylostoma caninum causes human eosinophilic enteritis
Symptoms include abdominal pain and intense local eosinophilia
Treatment: mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days) or albendazole (400 mg once)
Adult hookworms may be visualized by endoscopy

items.title: Understanding the Autoinfection Cycle and Hyperinfection Risk in Strongyloidiasis
items.content: Strongyloides stercoralis can complete its life cycle within a human host, allowing for autoinfection. This is particularly dangerous in immunocompromised individuals who may experience hyperinfection, leading to disseminated disease with high mortality.
keyPoints: S. stercoralis can replicate and cause ongoing cycles of autoinfection without exogenous exposure.
Hyperinfection occurs when host immunity is impaired, often due to glucocorticoid therapy or HTLV-1 infection.
Disseminated strongyloidiasis has a high mortality rate, especially in patients on glucocorticoids.

items.title: Diagnosing Strongyloidiasis: Techniques and Challenges
items.content: Diagnosis of strongyloidiasis can be challenging due to low larval output in uncomplicated cases. Multiple stool examinations, agar plate methods, PCR, and serologic testing are used to improve diagnostic sensitivity.
keyPoints: Single stool examination detects about one-third of uncomplicated infections; serial exams or agar plates increase sensitivity.
PCR is increasingly used for its higher sensitivity in detecting S. stercoralis.
Serologic tests are recommended for patients with potential exposure, especially those at risk of hyperinfection.

items.title: Clinical Features and Epidemiology of Strongyloidiasis
items.content: Strongyloidiasis often presents asymptomatically or with mild symptoms. In endemic areas like Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Brazil, it is more prevalent. Severe cases can lead to disseminated disease.
keyPoints: Many patients are asymptomatic; common symptoms include cutaneous manifestations like larva currens and abdominal pain.
Eosinophilia is common but may be absent in severe infections.
Endemic regions include tropical areas, Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, Brazil, and parts of the U.S. Southeast.

items.title: Treatment of Strongyloidiasis: Ivermectin vs. Albendazole
items.content: Even asymptomatic strongyloidiasis requires treatment due to the risk of hyperinfection and fatal dissemination. Ivermectin is preferred over albendazole for its efficacy, with a regimen of 200 µg/kg daily for 2 days compared to albendazole's 400 mg daily for 3 days.
keyPoints: Ivermectin (200 µg/kg daily for 2 days) is more effective than albendazole (400 mg daily for 3 days).
Treatment with ivermectin should be extended for at least 14 days after fecal exams are negative.
In immunocompromised hosts, repeat the course of ivermectin 2 weeks post-initial treatment.

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Trichuriasis
items.content: Trichuriasis, often asymptomatic, can cause gastrointestinal symptoms in heavy infections. Diagnosis involves detecting characteristic 50 x 20 µm lemon-shaped T. trichiura eggs in stool samples. Albendazole or mebendazole is used for treatment, with the addition of ivermectin significantly increasing cure rates.
keyPoints: Characteristic 50 x 20 µm lemon-shaped T. trichiura eggs are detected on microscopic examination of stool.
Albendazole (400 mg daily for 3 days) or mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days) is used for treatment.
Adding ivermectin (200 µg/kg daily for 3 doses) to benzimidazole drugs significantly increases cure rates.

items.title: Diagnosis of Pinworm Infection: Use of Tape Test
items.content: Pinworm infections are diagnosed by applying clear cellulose acetate tape to the perianal region in the morning and examining it microscopically for eggs, as conventional fecal ova and parasite microscopy is ineffective.
keyPoints: Eggs are detected using a tape test applied to the perianal area in the morning.
Microscopic examination reveals oval pinworm eggs measuring 55 x 25 µm, flattened on one side.
Conventional fecal ova and parasite microscopy cannot diagnose pinworm infections.

items.title: Treatment of Pinworm Infection: Recommended Regimen
items.content: Pinworm infection is treated with a single dose of mebendazole, albendazole, or pyrantel pamoate, followed by a repeat treatment after 2 weeks to address eggs and early-stage larvae. Household members should also be treated to prevent reinfection.
keyPoints: Mebendazole (100 mg once), albendazole (400 mg once), or pyrantel pamoate (11 mg/kg once) are used for treatment.
Treatment is repeated after 2 weeks due to the inability of drugs to kill eggs or early-stage larvae.
Household members should be treated to eliminate asymptomatic reservoirs and prevent reinfection.

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Trichostrongylus Infection
items.content: Trichostrongylus species, typically parasites in herbivores, can infect humans through ingestion of contaminated leafy vegetables. The worms mature directly into adults in the small bowel without migration. While most infections are asymptomatic, heavy infestations may cause mild anemia and eosinophilia. Diagnosis involves identifying larger eggs (85 x 115 µm) resembling hookworm eggs in stool examinations. Treatment options include mebendazole, albendazole, or pyrantel pamoate.
keyPoints: Humans acquire infection by ingesting larvae on contaminated vegetables.
Pathology similar to hookworm but with less blood ingestion.
Most infections are asymptomatic; heavy infections may cause mild anemia and eosinophilia.
Stool examination reveals eggs (85 x 115 µm) resembling hookworm eggs.
Treatment includes mebendazole, albendazole, or pyrantel pamoate.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.title: Management of Anisakiasis and Its Clinical Presentation
items.content: Anisakiasis results from ingesting raw or undercooked fish containing Anisakid larvae. The condition is more prevalent in regions where raw fish dishes are common, such as Japan, the Netherlands, and Chile. Symptoms include acute upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and can mimic Crohn's disease with intermittent abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, and fever. Diagnosis involves endoscopy or histopathologic examination. Larvae are killed by cooking to 60°C or freezing at -20°C for 3 days; no medical treatment exists, but surgical or endoscopic removal is curative.
keyPoints: Caused by ingestion of raw/undercooked fish with Anisakid larvae.
Symptoms: acute upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting; can mimic Crohn's disease.
Diagnosis via endoscopy or histopathologic examination.
Larvae killed by cooking to 60°C or freezing at -20°C for 3 days.
No medical treatment; surgical/endoscopic removal is curative.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical feature associated with infections due to major intestinal parasitic nematodes?
options: Contributes to malnutrition and impaired development
Protects individuals from all infectious diseases
Develops only after a single exposure
items.explanation: Intestinal nematode infections contribute to malnutrition, impaired physical and cognitive development, and diminished work capacity.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.question: Which of the following intestinal nematodes can be transmitted directly from person to person?
items.explanation: Strongyloides stercoralis and Enterobius vermicularis can be transmitted directly from person to person.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.question: What is a typical requirement for the life cycle of Ascaris lumbricoides, Trichuris trichiura, and hookworms?
options: Complete lifecycle within a single human host
items.explanation: Ascaris lumbricoides, Trichuris trichiura, and hookworms require a soil phase for development.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.question: What is the typical time frame between initial infection with Ascaris and detection of eggs in feces?
items.explanation: The time between initial infection and egg detection in the feces is typically between 2 and 3 months.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most commonly associated with the lung phase of larval migration?
items.explanation: During the lung phase, patients may develop an irritating nonproductive cough and burning substernal discomfort.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is most commonly used to identify Ascaris infection?
options: PCR of worm DNA extracted from stool
Microscopic detection of characteristic Ascaris eggs in fecal samples
items.explanation: Most cases of ascariasis can be diagnosed by microscopic detection of characteristic Ascaris eggs in fecal samples.

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for Ascaris?
options: Fecal contamination of soil and ingestion of infective eggs
items.explanation: Transmission typically occurs through fecally contaminated soil due to inadequate treatment of sewage or use of human feces as fertilizer.

items.question: Which medication for ascariasis should be avoided during pregnancy?
items.explanation: All medications listed are contraindicated in pregnancy.

items.question: What is the treatment approach for partial intestinal obstruction due to ascariasis?
items.explanation: Partial intestinal obstruction should be managed with nasogastric suction, IV fluid administration, and piperazine.

items.question: Which species of hookworm is more geographically restricted to areas of North Africa and northern Asia?
items.explanation: Ancylostoma duodenale is more geographically restricted to areas of North Africa and northern Asia.

items.question: What is the major consequence of chronic hookworm infection?
items.explanation: The major consequence of chronic hookworm infection is iron deficiency.

items.question: Which anthelmintic drug is recommended for treating mild iron-deficiency anemia associated with hookworm infection?
items.explanation: Mild iron-deficiency anemia can often be treated with oral iron supplementation alone.

items.question: What is a significant concern regarding the treatment of human hookworm infections?
options: Albendazole and mebendazole are becoming less effective
items.explanation: There is significant concern that the benzimidazoles (mebendazole and albendazole) are becoming much less effective against human hookworms.

items.question: Which species of canine hookworm has been identified as a cause of human eosinophilic enteritis?
items.explanation: Ancylostoma caninum, the canine hookworm, has been identified as a cause of human eosinophilic enteritis.

items.question: Which unique feature distinguishes S. stercoralis among helminths?
options: It can complete its life cycle and replicate within a human host.
It requires an external host to reproduce.
It cannot cause autoinfection in humans.
It is the only nematode that causes disseminated infection.
items.explanation: S. stercoralis can complete its life cycle and replicate within a human host, allowing for ongoing cycles of autoinfection.

items.question: What is the most common cutaneous manifestation in uncomplicated strongyloidiasis?
items.explanation: Recurrent urticaria, often involving the buttocks, is the most common cutaneous manifestation in uncomplicated strongyloidiasis.

items.question: What condition can lead to hyperinfection and disseminated strongyloidiasis?
items.explanation: Impairment of host cell-mediated immunity, especially with high-dose glucocorticoid therapy, can lead to hyperinfection and disseminated strongyloidiasis.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is sensitive for detecting uncomplicated strongyloidiasis?
options: Serologic testing for serum antibodies to Strongyloides antigens
items.explanation: An enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for serum antibodies to antigens of Strongyloides is a sensitive method for diagnosing uncomplicated infections.

items.question: Which treatment is more effective for strongyloidiasis?
options: Neither, as they have similar efficacy
items.explanation: Ivermectin is more effective than albendazole for treating strongyloidiasis.

items.question: What indicates a response to treatment in strongyloidiasis?
options: Increase in eosinophil count and antibody titer
Decrease in eosinophil count and antibody titer
No change in eosinophil count or antibody titer
items.explanation: A decrease in eosinophil count and antibody titer indicates a response to treatment.

items.question: What is the shape of Trichuris trichiura eggs?
items.explanation: Trichuris trichiura eggs are lemon-shaped.

items.question: Which of the following is the most common symptom of enterobiasis?
items.explanation: Perianal pruritus is the cardinal symptom of pinworm infections.

items.question: What diagnostic method is used to detect pinworm eggs?
items.explanation: Pinworm eggs are detected by applying clear cellulose acetate tape to the perianal region.

items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT recommended for enterobiasis?
items.explanation: Ivermectin is not mentioned as a treatment for pinworm infections.

items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT recommended for Trichostrongylus infection?
items.explanation: Trichostrongylus infections are treated with mebendazole, albendazole, or pyrantel pamoate.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.question: What is the typical size of Trichostrongylus eggs compared to hookworm eggs?
items.explanation: Trichostrongylus eggs are larger than hookworm eggs, measuring approximately $85 	imes 115	ext{ }μm$.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.question: Which of the following is a symptom that may occur with heavy Trichostrongylus infection?
items.explanation: Heavy infections with Trichostrongylus may lead to mild anemia.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.statement: Significant protective immunity to intestinal nematodes typically develops in humans.
items.explanation: Significant protective immunity to intestinal nematodes does not appear to develop in humans.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.statement: Eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE antibody levels are features of many helminth infections.
items.explanation: Eosinophilia and elevated serum IgE antibody levels are indeed features of many helminth infections.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · Intestinal Nematode Infections

items.statement: Ascaris eggs become infective immediately after being excreted in the feces.
items.explanation: Ascaris eggs become infective after several weeks of maturation in warm, moist soil.

items.statement: Eosinophilia is a prominent feature during the early transpulmonary migratory phase.
items.explanation: Eosinophilia develops during the symptomatic lung phase and subsides slowly over weeks.
Most hookworm infections are of light intensity and therefore clinically asymptomatic.

items.statement: The prepatent period for Ancylostoma spp. is shorter than that of Necator americanus.
items.explanation: The prepatent period may be longer with Ancylostoma spp.

items.statement: Severe hookworm disease with protein loss and malabsorption requires only deworming for treatment.
items.explanation: Severe hookworm disease necessitates nutritional support, oral iron replacement along with deworming.

items.statement: Ancylostoma braziliense can cause cutaneous larva migrans.
items.explanation: Both Ancylostoma caninum and Ancylostoma braziliense can cause cutaneous larva migrants ('creeping eruption').

items.statement: Eosinophilia is often absent in patients with disseminated strongyloidiasis.
items.explanation: In severely infected patients, eosinophilia is often absent due to the extensive nature of the infection.

items.statement: Strongyloides larvae are typically found in stool samples during uncomplicated infections.
items.explanation: In uncomplicated infections, few larvae are passed and single stool examinations detect only about one-third of cases.

items.statement: Ivermectin can be administered parenterally using veterinary preparations.
items.explanation: Ivermectin can indeed be given parenterally through single-patient expanded access protocols.

items.statement: Heavy infections with Trichuris trichiura never cause gastrointestinal symptoms.
items.explanation: Heavy infections may cause gastrointestinal symptoms such as abdominal pain and bloody or mucoid diarrhea.

items.statement: Pinworm eggs are released in feces.
items.explanation: Pinworm eggs are not released in feces; they are detected by the tape test.

items.statement: Autoinfection can occur due to perianal scratching.
items.explanation: Autoinfection results from perianal scratching and transport of infective eggs on the fingers or under the nails to the mouth.

items.statement: Anisakid larvae are killed by salting, marinating, or cold smoking.
items.explanation: Anisakid larvae in saltwater fish are not killed by salting, marinating, or cold smoking.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.statement: Capillariasis can lead to a severe wasting syndrome due to autoinfection.
items.explanation: Intestinal capillariasis caused by Capillaria philippinensis can lead to a severe wasting syndrome through autoinfection.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS

items.assertion: Ascaris lumbricoides is the largest intestinal nematode parasite of humans.
items.reason: It can reach up to 40 cm in length.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: Intestinal obstruction due to Ascaris is more common in adults.
items.reason: Adults have larger intestinal lumens compared to children.
items.explanation: A is false because obstruction is more common in children; R is also false.

items.assertion: Hookworm larvae can cause a pruritic maculopapular rash at the site of skin penetration.
items.reason: Infective hookworm larvae provoke 'ground itch' upon penetrating the skin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Hookworm eggs can be differentiated by light microscopy.
items.reason: Eggs of the three species are indistinguishable by light microscopy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.

items.assertion: Disseminated strongyloidiasis has a high mortality rate.
items.reason: It can lead to severe complications such as bacteremia, pneumonia, or meningitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why disseminated strongyloidiasis has a high mortality rate.

items.assertion: S. stercoralis is endemic in parts of the Southeast United States.
items.reason: It is commonly found in tropical areas and other warm, humid regions.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not specifically explain why S. stercoralis is endemic in the Southeast U.S.

items.assertion: Anisakiasis is often confused with Crohn's disease due to similar symptoms.
items.reason: The ingestion of Anisakis-derived proteins can elicit gastrointestinal responses resembling Crohn's disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why anisakiasis may be confused with Crohn's disease.
items.reference: Intestinal Nematode Infections · TRICHOSTRONGYLIASIS | ANISAKIASIS | CAPILLARIASIS
